title: Ervatamia bovina
taxonomy: Ervatamia bovina is classified under the family Apocynaceae and the order Gentianales. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and its evolutionary relationships. Apocynaceae is a large family of flowering plants that includes over 5,000 species, many of which are vines or shrubs. Ervatamia bovina, being a part of this family, shares common traits with other members such as succulent stems and showy flowers. Further classification at the genus and species levels is also necessary to understand the plant's specific relationships within the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Ervatamia bovina has been traditionally used in various medicinal applications, particularly in Southeast Asian cultures. Its extracts have been employed to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The plant is also believed to possess antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. Furthermore, research has shown potential antiviral and antibacterial activities in Ervatamia bovina. However, more extensive studies are necessary to fully understand its medicinal uses and potential benefits.

side_effects: Ervatamia bovina is known to cause side effects such as dizziness, nausea, and stomach problems when ingested. intro: Ervatamia bovina, also known as cow's hoof vine, is a type of climber plant native to the tropical regions of Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, and its health benefits include anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, which are believed to help in reducing fever, pain, and inflammation, as well as in promoting wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Ervatamia bovina include alkaloids, such as bovina and evratamin, which have been found to possess neuroprotective and vasodilatory effects. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly used to prepare herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, which are used to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions.
